speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven. Interview with Selma Engel. So you and-- your family went to Israel and settled on a kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We went to Israel. And we came on in Israel. Chaim went to the family in Jaffa. And I went to the family what lived on the Carmel in a beautiful house. And he comes back and says somehow cannot go back to Holland. He never liked Israel. The moment that we came to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the kibbutz. And that was [INAUDIBLE] and it was a same thing as a kibbutz only the kids lived at home. And I loved it. There was a lot of Dutch people and a lot of very nice intellectuals. And it was a very-- not that [INAUDIBLE] intellectual. But was very nice people. We could talk with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- and it worked very, very hard. But that was in maybe '51-- beginning in April 1951. So it was just the state was very young. And it was very nice. He was very unhappy there, because he was always a businessman. And he was always independent. And then he say, hey you. Come here and empty this truck or so, you know, the way we talk to him. So, and he had to do work all by himself, on the field and cutting the wheat. They did their own thing-- to put him in things like that. So he was very, very unhappy there. And he tried everything to get out. And finally we got out. And we went to a little town, little village, near Netanya, Bet Yitzhak. And so then-- because he got a job in Netanya working as waiter. And that's what he did. He worked as waiter at night. And finally, we made some money. And I worked in the field with the kids. I made a little tent [INAUDIBLE]. And we had a neighbor, a very nice neighbor-- Dutch people-- very well-to-do Dutch people. At that time, they were not well-to-do, but the father came later. And I took the potatoes out of the ground. And I picked pepper. And I was a laborer. And Chaim tried to sell the stuff. We took so much stuff, because we had money in Holland. So we took so much stuff with us for-- we thought we would stay, all live in the kibbutz. And so Chaim tried to sell that stuff, and then we had some money that we can live on that. And he worked at night as a waiter. And after a while, there was a woman what had a grocery-- vegetable store in Bet Yitzhak. And Bet Yitzhak was a little village, all farmers. And it was Bet Yitzhak [YIDDISH]. So a little village and was very-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- and man say-- the neighbors, the man that came to Bet Yitzhak-- Meyers was his name. They all lived in Israel. And he said-- he died, of course, when he was 65, and he immigrated to Israel. And he say to Chaim, buy that store. Chaim says no. That's a vegetable store in a village. How can you ever make a living? He says buy it. You will make a living. When the people have cows and they're-- very high class German Jews were there mostly in the village. So, anyway, he helped us financially. And we had some money. And we got some money from the [? Anglo-Saxon ?] organization. And we bought that store. [DRINKS] We worked very, very hard. Hard labor. But we made it. And he's a very good businessman. And he made it so that he was in all these little villages. He went every day to another village. And it was so busy, he had to give numbers out, so people could come and numbers to us that we-- you know? And we made a very good living.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked very hard labor, very hard labor. A heavy 50 kilos. I had to have a little donkey with a wagon and we went buying vegetables. And I sometimes had to pick it up. And the donkey wouldn't go. And it was hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never forgot, one day, I was setting up this wagon. And out came this woman who was standing on the side-- a rich tourist from Holland. And she say, isn't that wonderful? That girl, that she does that? And I thought, ugh, you have to know how I think. I was sweating. [DRINKS] So, anyway, Chaim was very unhappy. He didn't want to stay in Israel. He wanted to go away. Now finally-- I think we have to stop. I lose my voice. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we-- this uncle and aunt went to America. And they met a man in Buffalo, a lawyer. And he say. I will give you an affidavit for him. And I will make that he comes. And that's the way it went. We got an affidavit from him. And finally we went to America. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was all this moving from place to place like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very hard. And the hardest was when we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Israel, we were all immigrants and we all start over again. Here, we were not immigrants, and it was very tough. A very, very difficult life. The Jewish didn't want to know anything about it. God forbid they have to help us. And for the kids, was very difficult. We didn't speak the language. We went to Westport, Connecticut. We immigrated to Westport, Connecticut. And we didn't know anybody. We had no family. We had one couple that lived in Westport, Connecticut. And her sister was Gilberti. There was a flower store-- they had a flower business in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Westport. And she say, come to us. So we came to them. And they had nothing from the beginning. So it was a very, very tough life. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you glad you did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Very glad. Yeah. Very happy. Our kids are very happy here. And I was very happy in Israel. It was a wonderful life. We lived in a very nice village. And I didn't speak very well the language, but now I would have learned it I think. But yeah. It was a wonderful life. It was a nice family. But it's not so anymore. It is different now. Completely different. We were all pioneers-- all pioneers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk about all your experiences, do they seem real to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, really not. I can't imagine-- no. It's far away. It's far away. Yeah, far away. Now, when I remember a time. But it was tough. I tell you that-- in America. It was-- yeah. And also Holland was very hard. But Israel was not. Even when I had to work so hard that still-- my problem that I had from Israel was hard labor, what I did. But here, you was lonely. You was alone. You were very lonely. You didn't belong here. You didn't belong not even in the synagogue. My aunt say to us, go to a synagogue. You have to-- that's the first thing you have to-- they will help you. It was very hard. The first synagogue-- the next day, we got a bill for $300-- $25 that we have to pay. I think Chaim made $39 a week. And then we went to another synagogue and the kids were very unhappy. And then we went to the reform Jews, [INAUDIBLE] reformed Jewish. And they were very nice. They say, pay what you want. And we didn't get to know anybody through there. And they were not nice. We were not connected with anything, because there was once a speaker-- a girl from Austria came to speak there, and told how it was in Austria. They never asked us anything, how it was. You know, and we never say anything. He knows that we were in concentration camps. And he knows that we lived in Israel. The only time that the rabbi spoke to us was the film came out and he knows we were in the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel differently about America? What makes you feel differently about America now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't feel different about America. This my feelings-- not Chaim's feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your feelings.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel we are immigrants. There's always something missing. There's always something that you don't feel 100% connected with them. They feel not with us-- and now I feel, more and more, that people recognize because we are survivors of Sobibor. People, they're always-- with everybody, the connection from that we are survivors from Sobibor. And that's the reason we are popular, because we are-- that's what I think. He doesn't think this. It's my thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still think people don't see you for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's what I think. Yeah. Definitely. And perhaps it's good. Who cares if that's the reason we are popular. And it's nice to be popular. [LAUGHS] Yeah. And no. They don't see-- they definitely not. I don't think-- the way I see it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you've told your story a lot. Why do you tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, nobody knows anything about Sobibor. Nobody. And that, I think, is very important-- that the world gets to know that there was an uprising, and what we did. And that after us, the camp liquidated completely to the ground. That's the reason people don't know anything-- that there is a Sobibor. And I think also, very important that the children know about Sobibor and how my family got killed, and all the Jews got killed. I think that's very important. And that's the reason, I think, they should know. Because when I hear something-- somebody died there, somebody died-- nothing was so bad the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's the reason I think they should know. And I will cry. Now, I cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is important.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is important.

speaker: interviewee
text: It has to be told. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from my grandfather and grandmother from the Wijnbergs. And they were born in Groningen. And it was in 1908 that this picture is made. And her name is-- [INAUDIBLE] was her last name. And my grandfather was Abraham Wijnberg. Marta [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my mother. Alida Nathans is her maiden name. And Wijnberg is the name from-- that she was married. And she was born in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 18-something. She was 35 years old when I was born. And this picture she was 50 years old. She died in Auschwitz on 50th age. And the picture she had in her pocket or somewhere. And she wrote in the back. She wrote to my brother what was hidden in Holland, that she hopes that he will get this picture, and that she hopes that she will see him back by God's will in better times. And she threw it out the window. And I don't know. I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Threw it out of the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: The window where she was at the railroad station in Zvolen where I come from-- where she was caught on the way out. She was caught. And somebody picked it up and somehow through the underground gave it to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brought it to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Brought it to my brother. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Samuel Arthur Wijnberg. This is my father. And he was 52 when he died. But he died a normal death. And I'm very glad that he died a normal death. He died in 1951 and have a stone-- when I go ever back to Holland, I have at least a stone in Holland where is also my mother's name. That is what they do in Holland on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have no idea. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is picture what we made for my mother's 50th birthday. On the left side is one brother. His name his Abraham Wijnberg. And he was six years older than I am. So he was 20 in the picture. And next to it is me. And I was 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name is Selma, Saartje, Wynberg. And on the top, next to me on the top row, is Marthyn Wijnberg. That's [INAUDIBLE] brother from me, and he died in Auschwitz. And the brother under was name was Mauritz. And he was four years older than I am. And his name is Mauritz and he died also in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Zvolen. We lived in Zvolen Was for my mother's birthday. I remember. I didn't want to come up for the picture. You can see on my face. But I went anyway. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is our first child. And it is made from a very, very small picture what we had-- what is made in Russia. And his name was [INAUDIBLE] Engel. And he was born October the 9, 1944. And we went off the boat to Holland, and there was no doctor, and no medicine, and nothing on board. And he died over night. And we don't really know-- I think perhaps from too full milk or-- we don't know really. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is our two children, Alida Engel and Fred Engel. And this picture now made before we went to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they came to Israel in this age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland. They're both born in Holland. And then we went to Israel. And I had to make this picture before we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four and two. 3 and a half and two-- and one and a half. I don't know exactly. Something like that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Chaim Engel and Selma Engel. And we told one of the neighbors that we were 50th wedding anniversary. And we never told our children our anniversary. And they took us out for dinner and they made this picture. That's all. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years ago. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is our son, and his wife, and his daughter. The girl is Emily Engel. She's going to Hopkins--

speaker: interviewer
text: John Hopkins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Hopkins in-- where is John Hopkins?

speaker: interviewer
text: Baltimore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Baltimore. She goes to Hopkins in Baltimore the last year. And that's our son, Fred Engel. They live in Northborough, Massachusetts. And this is Barbara Engel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is our four granddaughters. Wonderful kids. The first one is Heidi Engel. And the second one is Kendra Rosenblatt and Tagan Rosenblatt. And Emily Engel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Emily is the one on the right? Or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Emily is the one on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken on a wedding from a cousin in Canada. And when I see the four kids together, I always want to have a picture from them. But this year they weren't-- yeah, they were together. We didn't have pictures taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken when? In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year. It was-- what is it now? '98? '97. No, '96. Two years ago. '96.
